blocks.text: Protect me, O God, for in you I take refuge.
Ps 16: God the supreme good. A declaration of loyalty to the one God of Israel, though others may worship many gods: such commitment brings great blessings.
I say to the Lord, “You are my Lord; I have no good apart from you.”
1–2 : Though a cry for rescue from an immediate danger, the verses are most probably a declaration of trust in a particular God—the —in traditional client‐patron language.
As for the holy ones in the land, they are the noble, in whom is all my delight.
3 : NRSV interprets the unclear verse as referring to Israelites whose fellowship one gains when one professes faith in the .
Those who choose another god multiply their sorrows; their drink offerings of blood I will not pour out or take their names upon my lips.
4 : are rare in biblical religion; see Ps 50.13, and cf. Ex 24.6.
The Lord is my chosen portion and my cup; you hold my lot.
The boundary lines have fallen for me in pleasant places; I have a goodly heritage.
5–6 : , and , a metaphor based on the distribution and possession of the land of Israel as in Josh 13–19. Possibly the psalmist was a priest; see Num 18.20; Josh 18.7.
I bless the Lord who gives me counsel; in the night also my heart instructs me.
I keep the Lord always before me; because he is at my right hand, I shall not be moved.
Therefore my heart is glad, and my soul rejoices; my body also rests secure.
For you do not give me up to Sheol, or let your faithful one see the Pit.
You show me the path of life. In your presence there is fullness of joy; in your right hand are pleasures forevermore.
7–11 : The response of the individual whose God is now the , giving praise (v. 8) and rejoicing in God’s nearness (vv. 9–11).
